items.title: While it makes sense for the US, Iran and Russia to keep oil prices high, it makes no sense to have inflation ruin most others and in fact hurt the US...
items.description_html:
While it makes sense for the US, Iran and Russia to keep oil prices high, it makes no sense to have inflation ruin most others and in fact hurt the US.

I believe we will see lower prices on crude, and a return to availability soon. It makes sense for a longer term policy response... Like to change fpi taxation, to introduce duties on end use goods, to push local manufacturing, refining of materials etc, and to explore local crude oil resources.

But it doesn't make sense to unnecessarily hike rates. The govt has done an amazing job managing shortages but it's time to build the next India. Semiconductors, machinery, cold storage, and so on.

Rbis work with the rupee right now is actually good, and remember that if it buys dollars on the way in, it must sell them on the way out. It's not selling enough, and doesn't have the insti linkages that say boj or boe has, but in general the response has been decent. We still run a surplus on liquidity. They should top this up with a distribution of their own profits properly, regardless of what our govt needs.

I would stay prepared for short term pain but it does look like peak pessimism on India. I just don't think a country of smart hungry people will just wiffle away all the economic growth because some uncles decide there's nothing here. We grow because we are ambitious, sometimes despite our govt and the bureaucracy, and the next decade is going to be good here. Two years ago it was just india and no one else. Today's everyone else but India. Both will prove to be wrong.

items.title: RT ANI: #WATCH | Mumbai, Maharashtra: National Stock Exchange of India (NSE) Managing Director and CEO Ashishkumar Chauhan attends the CareEdge Debt M...
items.description_html:
RT ANI
#WATCH | Mumbai, Maharashtra: National Stock Exchange of India (NSE) Managing Director and CEO Ashishkumar Chauhan attends the CareEdge Debt Market Summit 2026.

He says, “There is an old saying among bridge builders: the structure is only as strong as its weakest pillar. India's financial architecture has built an impressive equity market infrastructure over the last three and a half decades. The corporate bond market is the pillar that must now be strengthened to the same standard…The question before us is how far we have progressed and how much further we need to go. The progress is real and measurable. India's outstanding corporate bond market has expanded at an annualised pace of 13.6% to ₹59.1 lakh crore in Financial Year 2026, growing faster than the overall bank credit growth of 12.1% over the same period. As a share of GDP, corporate bonds now stand at 17.1%, up from 11.7% in Financial Year 2012… Corporate bonds represent 21.5% of the overall domestic debt market. It's meaningful, but not yet transformative…”

items.title: Watch Live: https://lnkd.in/dyzXmEzH As India charts its path towards becoming a Viksit Bharat and a USD 30 trillion economy by 2047, the role of a de...
items.description_html:
Watch Live: https://lnkd.in/dyzXmEzH

As India charts its path towards becoming a Viksit Bharat and a USD 30 trillion economy by 2047, the role of a deep, resilient, and diversified financial system has never been more critical. The debt market, often underappreciated, will be central to this journey. A strong bond market can unlock long-term capital, reduce reliance on banks, lower financing costs, and enhance efficient resource allocation, especially in a world facing rising geopolitical fragmentation and global uncertainty.

While India’s debt market has already supported economic growth, the next phase requires greater accessibility, innovation, and reforms to truly position it among the world’s most developed markets.

The CareEdge Debt Market Summit 2026 on May 26 comes at an especially opportune moment, as India’s growth ambitions and evolving global dynamics make strengthening debt markets more critical than ever. It brings together policymakers, regulators, global investors, bankers, and market experts to shape the way forward.

What to expect:

• Insightful addresses from leaders across SEBI, NSE, and CareEdge
• Exclusive release of the CareEdge Debt Market Report

Expert discussions on:

• Can India’s debt capital market finance its growth ambitions?
• Repricing risk amid global fragmentation
• Emerging trends and opportunities in securitisation
• Perspectives from industry leaders across finance, banking, and global markets
• Valuable networking with key stakeholders shaping India’s capital markets ecosystem

This summit is more than a conversation. It’s a step towards shaping the future of India’s financial architecture.

Services imports increased 7.4% YoY, following a contraction in November (-2.04%), contributing to the net expansion.

items.title: In November, U.S. exports grew 6.1% YoY, slowing from 15.8% in October, while imports rose 6.5% month-on-month but stayed lower YoY. Exports to China ...
items.description_html:
In November, U.S. exports grew 6.1% YoY, slowing from 15.8% in October, while imports rose 6.5% month-on-month but stayed lower YoY.

Exports to China fell for the 11th consecutive month, with China’s share of U.S. exports dropping to 3.8%. Exports to Taiwan and India rose YoY.

items.description_html: December’s pickup followed a 7.2% expansion in November, a clear improvement over the 3.7% growth a year earlier. The acceleration was supported by strength in manufacturing, mining, and electricity. Consumer durables and infrastructure goods also posted double-digit gains.

items.description_html:
Truly incredible.

Micron stock, $MU, is now up another +6% in overnight trading, adding another +$60 billion in market cap in hours.

Just 12 months ago, the entire stock was worth less than $70 billion.

Including the gain during regular trading hours, Micron has now added +$220 BILLION today.

This makes Micron the 12th largest public company in the world.

items.title: Leveraged trading in Asia is more popular than ever: The 2x Leveraged SK Hynix ETF, listed in Hong Kong, has attracted +$1.3 billion in year-to-date i...
items.description_html:
Leveraged trading in Asia is more popular than ever:

The 2x Leveraged SK Hynix ETF, listed in Hong Kong, has attracted +$1.3 billion in year-to-date inflows.

As a result, total ETF assets under management (AUM) are up to $8.0 billion, the highest on record, making it the world's largest single-stock leveraged ETF by assets.

This fund’s AUM has more than TRIPLED over the last 3 months.

Furthermore, the 2x leveraged Samsung ETF has attracted +$1.3 billion in inflows so far in 2026.

This exceeds the year-to-date inflows of comparable 2x leveraged ETFs tracking Tesla, $TSLA, and Microsoft, $MSFT.

Now, SK Hynix and Samsung are the two largest South Korean stocks, together accounting for nearly 50% of the KOSPI index, which has a total market cap of $4.5 trillion.

Asian retail investors are rushing into leveraged chip stock bets like never before.

items.title: AI is increasingly driving market profitability: The S&P 500's net profit margin excluding financials is up to a record ~15%. At the same time, the S&...
items.description_html:
AI is increasingly driving market profitability:

The S&P 500's net profit margin excluding financials is up to a record ~15%.

At the same time, the S&P 500's net margin excluding the Magnificent 7 and tech is down to ~8%, near the lowest since the 2020 pandemic.

This marks a ~7 percentage point gap between tech and non-tech sectors, the widest on record.

This comes as margins for companies outside of tech have been trending down since 2022.

Meanwhile, Magnificent 7 and tech firms have seen a rapid increase in margins over the last several quarters.

AI is all that matters right now.

items.title: Defensive sectors are experiencing historic relative weakness: The S&P 500 utilities sector relative to the S&P 500 is down to a record low ratio of 0...
items.description_html:
Defensive sectors are experiencing historic relative weakness:

The S&P 500 utilities sector relative to the S&P 500 is down to a record low ratio of 0.06.

This ratio has declined -40% since the 2022 bear market.

Over this period, the S&P 500 has rallied +106% while the utilities sector index has increased just +38%.

By comparison, during the 2008 Financial Crisis, the ratio was +200% higher, at ~0.18.

Meanwhile, consumer staples, healthcare, and utilities collectively now account for just ~15% of the S&P 500’s market cap, the lowest since at least the 1970s.

Defensive sectors are historically unpopular right now.

items.title: China is seeing massive capital outflows: An estimated $1 trillion in capital flowed out of China in 2025, the largest annual outflow since records be...
items.description_html:
China is seeing massive capital outflows:

An estimated $1 trillion in capital flowed out of China in 2025, the largest annual outflow since records began in 2006.

Capital outflows have more than DOUBLED since 2021.

This comes as Chinese investors have moved funds into overseas equities through offshore brokers, particularly into the US and Hong Kong markets.

In response, China imposed restrictions on cross-border stock trading on May 22nd, ordering all illegal accounts to be liquidated within 2 years.

Furthermore, the country fined three offshore online brokers, Hong Kong-based Futu, Singapore-based Tiger Brokers and Longbridge Securities, a combined $330 million for offering Chinese investors access to foreign stock markets without regulatory approval.

China is tightening capital controls as outflows intensify.

items.title: US Treasuries are experiencing the worst bear market in history: The US Treasury Total Return Index has now been in a drawdown for 69 consecutive mont...
items.description_html:
US Treasuries are experiencing the worst bear market in history:

The US Treasury Total Return Index has now been in a drawdown for 69 consecutive months, the longest streak in over 100 years of data.

The previous record stretch that ended in 2019 lasted for ~30 months.

This is also only the 3rd time in history that a drawdown has exceeded 20 months.

During the current drawdown, the US Treasury Total Return Index fell as much as -18% from 2020 to 2022.

Since then, it has recovered some of its losses, but it is still down -6% since 2020.

Meanwhile, the 20+ year Treasury ETF, $TLT, is down -40% since its April 2020 peak.

US bonds are more unpopular than ever.

items.title: BREAKING: Commercial and industrial loans at US commercial banks jumped +$211 billion YoY in the week ending May 13th, to $2.89 trillion, the highest ...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Commercial and industrial loans at US commercial banks jumped +$211 billion YoY in the week ending May 13th, to $2.89 trillion, the highest since June 2020.

This also marks the 3rd largest increase since April 2023.

Year-to-date alone, commercial and industrial loans have risen +$185 billion.

Loans are now just $180 billion below the May 2020 peak of $3.07 trillion.

This comes amid rising inflation concerns and a weakening labor market which have increased economic uncertainty.

Furthermore, companies are rushing to secure lower borrowing costs as surging energy prices fuel renewed Fed rate hike fears.

Corporate America is borrowing at a pace not seen in years.

items.title: BREAKING: The total market cap of Taiwan's stock market has risen to a record $4.95 trillion, surpassing India's $4.92 trillion for the first time, ma...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The total market cap of Taiwan's stock market has risen to a record $4.95 trillion, surpassing India's $4.92 trillion for the first time, making Taiwan the world's 5th largest stock market.

Taiwan's market cap has more than DOUBLED since mid-2024, fueled by the AI-led surge in semiconductor stocks.

Over this time, Taiwan Semiconductor, $TSM, which accounts for 42% of Taiwan's total market value, has rallied +130%.

On the other hand, India’s market cap has dropped -12% over the same period, driven by record foreign outflows due to expensive valuations and a weakening Rupee.

To put this into perspective, India ranks as the world’s 6th largest economy, with a GDP of $4.15 trillion, exceeding Taiwan’s GDP of $977 billion by 325%.

Taiwan has become a global economic power.

items.title: Active managers are significantly underperforming in 2026: Just 25% of large-cap active equity mutual funds are beating the S&P 500 year-to-date, the ...
items.description_html:
Active managers are significantly underperforming in 2026:

Just 25% of large-cap active equity mutual funds are beating the S&P 500 year-to-date, the lowest reading since 2021.

This percentage is on track for its 4th consecutive annual decline.

Even at the peak of the 2008 Financial Crisis, 40% of active managers were still outperforming the index.

Yet before the Iran War began, almost 50% were outperforming the S&P 500.

However, as investors rotated aggressively into technology and semiconductor stocks during the conflict, that edge evaporated almost entirely, as these are sectors where active managers tend to be underweight.

Missing the AI trade is incredibly costly.

items.title: US consumer stocks are extremely weak: The equal-weighted US consumer discretionary index relative to the S&P 500 is down to 0.07, the lowest in at le...
items.description_html:
US consumer stocks are extremely weak:

The equal-weighted US consumer discretionary index relative to the S&P 500 is down to 0.07, the lowest in at least 20 years.

Since 2021, this ratio has fallen -42%, dropping below both the 2020 and 2008 lows.

Over this period, the S&P 500 has surged +60%, outperforming the equal-weighted US consumer discretionary index by 10x, which has increased just +6%.

Meanwhile, US Consumer Sentiment fell another -5.0 points in May after falling -3.5 points in April, to 44.8, an all-time low.

By comparison, the S&P 500 is up +17% since March 30th and closed on Friday just 0.5% below its all-time high.

The stock market is thriving, but the average American consumer is not.

items.title: I hosted @kanodiaankit12 on the podcast again. Last time we spoke, most micro caps were bleeding. This time, we went somewhere I didn't expect - a ful...
items.description_html:
I hosted @kanodiaankit12 on the podcast again.

Last time we spoke, most micro caps were bleeding. This time, we went somewhere I didn't expect - a full breakdown of consumption as a sector.

Food. Retail. Cinema. Amusement parks. Luggage. Hotels.
45 minutes of pure insights.

But the thing that hit me hardest had nothing to do with stock picks.

He said: "You're not an allocator of money. You're first an allocator of time."
Every investable rupee he has goes into equity. No FDs. No debt funds. Because he's clear he's still in asset creation mode.

His portfolio? 15 stocks max. His co-founder runs 5.

Concentrated. Intentional. Not 50 stocks dressed up as "diversification."
Most people in their 20s and 30s obsess over asset allocation. That's a preservation mindset. You can't preserve what you haven't built yet.

He also admitted the market has humbled him on stocks he's held for 3 years. No spin.

Even said he's considering selling one position just to see how he feels about the business without owning it.

That kind of self-awareness is rare.

Watch our full conversation here - https://youtu.be/TasQSPIhg08?si=9Kf6cDMVaGB3Kyb2

items.title: RT ValueEquity: Entero healthcare , after giving it lot of thought today , I am still choosing to sit on the sidelines and not go ahead with initiatio...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
Entero healthcare , after giving it lot of thought today , I am still choosing to sit on the sidelines and not go ahead with initiation , I think the growth guidance for next year 23% (without acquisitions ) is not good enough for the valuation multiple

1) they are going to focus on organic business which is actually going to grow on a reduced multiplier with the context of IPM (1.7 to 1.4)

2)It is actually going to be their first year in the last many years that they will be putting the brakes on acquisitions after IPO cash is over hence that will re calibrate the over all growth business and hence a lot of focus will be on margin and lesser over all growth

3) I personally think FY27 May be a year of calibration for them to come to a stronger FY28 with med tech contributions being higher and improved cash as well

4) minority interest - their acquisitions contracts have a call option for 3-5 years of full buyouts until which they will still not have the flow to PAT as we would like , and finance costs are expected to decline only after 3-4 quarters hence missing the flow through , but they will do well on the margins and market may like that

5) I actually think Entero will be a more investable business for me in F28 with a full year of calibration and maybe better valuations plus an organic tested model as well as I still think valuations are high

And again these are just my opinions and what I think about why I would not initiate , I can go wrong as well and the stock may still do well but the equation just wouldn’t add up for me , and hence still sit out

for me for now , phenomenal business model , good management , good terminal value , great industry , just bad entry valuations adjusted to growth equation 😭, hence sitting out

Disc : no positions

items.title: Entero healthcare , after giving it lot of thought today , I am still choosing to sit on the sidelines and not go ahead with initiation , I think the ...
items.description_html:
Entero healthcare , after giving it lot of thought today , I am still choosing to sit on the sidelines and not go ahead with initiation , I think the growth guidance for next year 23% (without acquisitions ) is not good enough for the valuation multiple

1) they are going to focus on organic business which is actually going to grow on a reduced multiplier with the context of IPM (1.7 to 1.4)

2)It is actually going to be their first year in the last many years that they will be putting the brakes on acquisitions after IPO cash is over hence that will re calibrate the over all growth business and hence a lot of focus will be on margin and lesser over all growth

3) I personally think FY27 May be a year of calibration for them to come to a stronger FY28 with med tech contributions being higher and improved cash as well

4) minority interest - their acquisitions contracts have a call option for 3-5 years of full buyouts until which they will still not have the flow to PAT as we would like , and finance costs are expected to decline only after 3-4 quarters hence missing the flow through , but they will do well on the margins and market may like that

5) I actually think Entero will be a more investable business for me in F28 with a full year of calibration and maybe better valuations plus an organic tested model as well as I still think valuations are high

And again these are just my opinions and what I think about why I would not initiate , I can go wrong as well and the stock may still do well but the equation just wouldn’t add up for me , and hence still sit out

for me for now , phenomenal business model , good management , good terminal value , great industry , just bad entry valuations adjusted to growth equation 😭, hence sitting out

Raising close to INR 18 Cr with IPO opening today

items.title: The positive side of India is often ignored. India produces world class things across many areas: 1. Automobiles India is one of the strongest 2 wheel...
items.description_html:
The positive side of India is often ignored.

India produces world class things across many areas:

1. Automobiles

India is one of the strongest 2 wheeler manufacturing ecosystems in the world

Companies like Hero MotoCorp, Bajaj Auto, TVS Motor & Royal Enfield have built strong products for India & export markets

Indian companies supply to global OEMs across engines, forgings, castings, wiring harnesses, tyres, batteries, lighting, braking systems & precision components

Many Indian auto ancillary companies are now part of global supply chains

2. Pharmaceuticals & Vaccines

India is called the pharmacy of the world for a reason

India is the largest global supplier of generic medicines & accounts for around 20% of global generic medicine supply by volume. It also supplies medicines and vaccines to more than 200 countries

India is one of the most important vaccine manufacturing hubs globally

3. Hospitals & healthcare delivery

India has world class private hospitals, especially in cardiac care, oncology, organ transplants, orthopaedics, IVF, robotic surgery & multi-speciality healthcare

The biggest strength is quality healthcare at much lower cost compared to developed markets

This is why medical tourism is booming in India

4. Hotels & hospitality

India has world class hospitality brands & luxury hotels

Taj, Oberoi, ITC, Leela & several premium hotel chains offer service quality that can compete globally

Indian hospitality is one area where service standards are world class

5. Global Capability Centres (GCC's)

Global companies are increasingly using India not only for back office work, but for product development, engineering, analytics, cybersecurity and global operations

India’s GCC ecosystem is expected to cross 2,100 centres by FY26, employing millions & generating around $100 Bn in REV

6. Digital public infrastructure

UPI is one of India’s most successful world class products

It has become one of the largest real time payment systems globally

7. Space technology

ISRO has shown that India can execute complex space missions at very low cost

Chandrayaan, Mars Orbiter Mission & satellite launch capabilities have proved India’s engineering depth

8. Defence manufacturing

India is improving fast in defence production

Missiles, radars, naval systems, artillery, drones, defence electronics & warships are areas where India is building capability

Companies like HAL, BEL, Bharat Dynamics, Mazagon Dock, Cochin Shipyard and private defence players are part of this ecosystem

9. Railways & Metro systems

India has built one of the largest railway networks in the world

Metro systems in Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune and other cities are improving urban transport

Vande Bharat is also an example of India’s improving rail manufacturing capability

10. Renewable energy

India is one of the largest renewable energy markets globally

India is also building domestic manufacturing in solar modules, cells, inverters & batteries

Reliance's Jamnagar complex is the largest single site refinery in the world

India has become a credible alternative to China in specialty chemicals

Indian companies are gaining share in pharma intermediates, agrochemicals, fluorochemicals, dyes, pigments, polymers and specialty materials

India has a deep textile ecosystem across cotton, yarn, fabrics, home textiles, garments and technical textiles

Companies from India supply to global retailers and brands

India is one of the most important diamond cutting and polishing hubs in the world

Surat is a global diamond processing centre

India is one of the largest producers of milk, spices, rice, wheat, pulses, fruits and vegetables

Indian spices, basmati rice, tea, coffee, dairy and processed foods have global demand

India has built a world class capital market infrastructure

Talent pool is one of the biggest reasons global companies keep expanding in India

India’s strength is that it can produce at scale, at low cost, with improving quality

Also, one underrated positive is the domestic convenience India offers

From affordable domestic help to quick grocery delivery, food delivery, repair services, drivers, laundry, salons, tailoring and local service networks - India offers a level of everyday convenience that is difficult to find in many developed countries

Yes, there are issues - infrastructure gaps, pollution, bureaucracy and execution challenges

India is not perfect, but there are many things India does well and the positive side also deserves attention

Many people keep highlighting the negatives, but this was a key attempt to shift some focus towards the positives.

items.title: Aerospace/Precision manufacturing is one of the most exciting themes Many companies in this space are getting re-rated because India is slowly moving ...
items.description_html:
Aerospace/Precision manufacturing is one of the most exciting themes

Many companies in this space are getting re-rated because India is slowly moving up the manufacturing value chain

Earlier, India was seen more as a low cost manufacturing base

Now, the opportunity is moving towards high precision components, aerospace assemblies, defence parts, turbine components, critical engineering products & global OEM supply chains

The reason this theme is attractive is because qualification cycles are long

Once a company gets approved by a global OEM, the relationship can become sticky

Many of these companies are already trading at very expensive valuations

In some cases, the market is already discounting many years of strong growth, margin expansion & flawless execution.

Now execution has to justify the valuation.

items.title: Not all Capex is the same As investors, the first question should always be: Is this maintenance capex or growth capex? If a company says maintenance ...
items.description_html:
Not all Capex is the same

As investors, the first question should always be:
Is this maintenance capex or growth capex?

If a company says maintenance capex is going to be much higher than expected, that is usually negative.

Why? Because it means the company needs to spend more just to protect existing capacity, maintain current operations and keep the business running.

In that case, higher capex reduces free cash flow without necessarily creating additional growth.

But if the company says demand is strong and it needs to build more capacity to serve that demand, then the interpretation is completely different.
That is growth capex.

So the key is not whether capex is increasing.
The key is why capex is increasing.

items.title: This is the exact context behind why EPS growth matters more than headline profit growth In this case, the headline numbers look strong at first glanc...
items.description_html:
This is the exact context behind why EPS growth matters more than headline profit growth

In this case, the headline numbers look strong at first glance

Net Profit increased from 31 Cr to 45 Cr, that looks great at 45% growth

But once we look at profit attributable to shareholders, the picture changes

Profit for EPS increased from 25.69 Cr to 28 Cr, that is 9% growth

EPS also increased from 5.90 to 6.44, again 9% growth

So the company may show strong headline Net Profit growth, but shareholders are not getting the full benefit

The main reason is the higher minority share / non-controlling interest

That is why reported Net Profit growth & EPS growth can look very different

The key question should always be:
Is the company growing profits faster than dilution & minority leakage?

That is why in this case, absolute Net Profit growth looks strong, but EPS growth tells the real story.


Shiladitya: PAT after Minority Interest
When looking at P&L statement in the quarterly results,
always look at PAT after minority interest and not just the consolidated PAT.

The consolidated PAT considers the entire group profit - So, if a company has 55% ownership of a subsidiary, the

items.title: Taiwan surpassing India in Market cap is not about population, It is about profit pools Taiwan has TSMC, It has become one of the most important compa...
items.description_html:
Taiwan surpassing India in Market cap is not about population, It is about profit pools

Taiwan has TSMC, It has become one of the most important companies in the world because almost every major AI company depends on its advanced chip manufacturing

As AI capex exploded globally, money started flowing aggressively into semiconductor & AI hardware winners

India remains a domestic growth story, it is more diversified across banks, IT, consumption, industrials, autos, pharma and energy

That concentration is risky in normal times, but during an AI supercycle, it becomes a massive advantage

Taiwan & Korea owns a large part of the AI hardware profit pool

And right now, AI hardware is attracting more global capital than most other themes

items.title: RT DepressedBergman: Satyajit Ray on how audience from other countries can connect with his movies: "There are obvious risks in exposing an audience t...
items.description_html:
RT DepressedBergman
Satyajit Ray on how audience from other countries can connect with his movies:

"There are obvious risks in exposing an audience to a wholly alien culture. There was an American lady, for instance, who was so upset by the spectacle of Indians eating with their fingers that she had to leave the theatre as soon as the second dinner episode commenced. But such squeamishness is not common. If details confuse, or offend, or go over the head, there is always the human element to fall back upon.

In 'Apur Sansar' (1959), Apu attends the wedding of a friend’s sister. The bridegroom (this is an arranged marriage) arrives and is found to be touched in the head. With the bride’s mother refusing to give away her daughter, the marriage seems on the verge of being abandoned. If it doesn’t take place at the appointed hour, the bride would be cursed and made unmarriageable for life. The crisis is averted at the last moment by Apu offering to step in as the groom. A Western viewer ignorant of orthodox Hindu customs must find the episode highly bizarre. But since Apu himself finds it so, and since his action is prompted by compassion, the viewer accepts it on moral grounds, though given no opportunity to weigh the pros and cons of a seemingly irrational practice.

Faced with a film from an alien culture, swamped by details which evoke no responses in him, a viewer will often find that the only aspect which offers a common ground is the moral one. Broad concepts of good and evil, right and wrong, do not vary much from culture to culture. Human behaviour also falls within patterns which are largely familiar and predictable. Abnormal situations may affect such behaviour as, say, in Hitler’s Germany; but as long as a film makes the context clear, a viewer is unlikely to lose his bearings. This can happen only when the development of the story itself turns on social and historical factors of which the viewer has no knowledge."

("Deep Focus- Reflections on Cinema", Satyajit Ray, 2011)

P.S: On this day, 67 years ago, 'Apur Sansar' was released in India.

items.title: RT Kayezad E Adajania: DO SIPs WORK? Of the 295 actively managed, diversified equity schemes with a two-year track record, 26% haven’t made any money...
items.description_html:
RT Kayezad E Adajania
DO SIPs WORK?

Of the 295 actively managed, diversified equity schemes with a two-year track record, 26% haven’t made any money in the last two years if you had started your SIP then, according to ACE MF data. Nearly 54% have failed to beat 5% returns. Just 1% of schemes have made returns in excess of 10% if you had started your SIP two years back.

These are precisely the times when investors question the veracity and wisdom of SIPs. Which is where our annual @ET_Wealth - @CrisilLimited annual SIP Study 2026 comes in.

If you continue your SIPs for 10 years, you won’t lose money. But the probability of incurring losses starts to decline after 4 years.

+ This year we have also analysed how long it takes for SIPs to recover after market crash. This is for those who panic and stop their SIPs in turbulence.

But the bigger story is how investors actually behave during a crisis. Our cover story features three individuals who didn’t stop their SIPs despite a personal crisis, and two who did and paid the price.

My story in this week’s @ET_Wealth. Let me know in the comments how you’ve reacted during a crisis; I would love to hear your stories.

-Gross FDI at $94.5 billion in FY26 vs $80.6 billion in FY25

- Net FDI at $7.7 billion vs $1 billion.

As Grant Thornton India’s Rishi Shah said “the more interesting story lies in the gap” and that disaggregated data needs studying. One option is that Indian corporates are maturing and looking overseas, he said. But an equally important question is whether India’s investment architecture is being scaled adequately for what’s arriving and not what’s receding.

Rate hikes are not the first port of call for the RBI to defend the rupee even now, @kalrajs23 and I report.

Instead they will watch for pass through of higher oil prices into core and headline inflation to judge timing and extent of rate hikes.

Rationale:

🔸many other options remain to defend rupee, hardly any have been activated.
🔸 rate defence of rupee will need steep hikes (not one 50bps move) and that will kill growth.
🔸 Inflation still seen within tolerance band.

Read the thinking below (not just the headline!)

Neuland vs Anthem Bio vs Divis - Whats the difference in their peptide strategy

Divi's is the protected-amino-acid + innovator fragment specialist that has explicitly refused generic peptides, prioritizing margin over TAM

Neuland is climbing from fragments toward full peptide NCE APIs as a process-chemistry CDMO, with its 2,000L SPPS Module 1 commissioning July 2026 but no signed commercial contract yet

Anthem is the only credible generic GLP-1 play, uniquely integrating recombinant P29 fermentation with synthesis and targeting semaglutide commercialization in 6-8 months

items.title: RT Pulse Of Profit: Jain Resource Recycling Ltd corrected nearly 40% recently amid concerns around weak operating cash flow and rising receivables. @T...
items.description_html:
RT Pulse Of Profit
Jain Resource Recycling Ltd corrected nearly 40% recently amid concerns around weak operating cash flow and rising receivables.

@Tijori1

But management says the issue is largely accounting-related, not operational 👇

Key clarification from the concall:

• Reported cash flow was impacted due to NFRA accounting requirements
• Receivables discounted with banks now need to be shown separately instead of netting off
• Management claims actual cash flows remain healthy
Other important points:
• Copper prices & volumes up 26% in recent months
• No fresh bank borrowing in the last 4 months
• Working capital funded through internal accruals
• Inventory levels already reduced from Q3 to Q4
• Targeting reduction in working capital cycle from 66 days to below 60 days
• Receivable days currently managed at 15–18 days
Management expects working capital to turn positive from Q2 onwards.

Important to watch now:
👉 Whether upcoming quarters validate management commentary through improving cash generation and lower working capital intensity.

items.title: Pre-Market Update!! From power grids to tyres, defence to retail Indian corporates are betting big on India's growth story. • Vedanta's Chairman has ...
items.description_html:
Pre-Market Update!!

From power grids to tyres, defence to retail Indian corporates are betting big on India's growth story.

• Vedanta's Chairman has announced an ambitious target of 20,000 MW through brownfield expansion, backed by a coal block. The conglomerate is urging the government for a long-term lease to facilitate large-scale investment commitments.

• Union Bank of India has received board approval to raise ₹8,000 crore via a blended financing approach, combining both debt instruments and equity, aimed at bolstering the lender's capital base and supporting future growth.

• RedTape is charting an aggressive retail footprint expansion for FY27, planning to launch 200–250 new outlets annually. The brand is placing particular emphasis on deepening its presence across Southern and Western Indian markets.

• Pakka Limited has greenlit the issuance of secured Non-Convertible Debentures aggregating up to ₹540 crore on a private placement basis a notable fundraising move for a company currently carrying a market capitalisation of just ₹404 crore.

• BEL has secured fresh defence and electronics contracts valued at ₹608 crore, spanning critical segments including communication systems, radar technology, avionics equipment, and advanced military simulators, further strengthening its order pipeline.

• JK Tyre's board has approved a phased capacity expansion worth ₹4,980 crore for Truck & Bus Radial and Passenger Car Radial tyres at its Chennai and Vikrant plants, building upon an existing base of 210 lakh tyres per annum.

• KEC International has bagged fresh orders aggregating ₹1,303 crore across multiple verticals. Notably, its T&D division clinched 400/220 kV substation and transmission line projects across Southern India, bolstering its already robust order book.

• Adani Green Energy has commissioned a 1,990 MWh Battery Energy Storage System alongside a 50 MW solar facility at Khavda, Gujarat, marking a significant stride in India's renewable energy and grid-storage infrastructure.

• Pondy Oxides has sanctioned a ₹200 crore copper recycling facility in Tamil Nadu with a capacity of 36,000 MTPA. The plant, deploying pyro and electro-refining technology, is slated for commissioning by December 2026.

• Transrail Lighting has sanctioned a ₹203 crore capital expenditure plan focused on debottlenecking operations, upgrading machinery, and scaling capacity to meet the rising demands of its expanding order book.

• E2E Rail Infra has secured a ₹216 crore EPC contract for electrification, signalling, and telecommunication works on a railway track, reinforcing its growing foothold in India's rapidly expanding rail infrastructure sector.

• Marine Electricals has bagged a ₹96.4 crore contract from Princeton Digital Group for the supply and installation of power distribution systems, adding a significant order to its growing infrastructure portfolio.

Ex Governor YV Reddy once said: "As a central banker, I am paid to worry."
Bimal Jalan put it differently. "In the morning I wake up wondering what to worry about next."
@SmalhotraRBI

items.title: The vote was also an important step toward making Swiggy an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC). As part of the proposed changes to the Article...
items.description_html:
The vote was also an important step toward making Swiggy an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC).

As part of the proposed changes to the Articles of Association (AoA), foreign investors SoftBank and Accel would have lost their rights to nominate board members at Swiggy.

Those board seats were set to go to the management team, giving Swiggy greater local control.

Foreign ownership in Swiggy has already come down to 52.19%, aided by the company’s Rs 10,000 crore QIP in December.

Over 80% of the QIP was subscribed to by domestic investors, including mutual funds.

However, Swiggy’s share price has fallen more than 33% since the QIP.

No wonder mutual fund investors are not happy right now.


Madhav Chanchani: 🚨 Swiggy’s management team has lost a shareholder vote to appoint CFO Rahul Bothra and co-founder Phani Kishan Addepalli to the company’s board.

The appointments will not take place now.

The proposal was part of changes to the Articles of Association (AoA) that would have

items.title: 🚨 Swiggy’s management team has lost a shareholder vote to appoint CFO Rahul Bothra and co-founder Phani Kishan Addepalli to the company’s board. ...
items.description_html:
🚨 Swiggy’s management team has lost a shareholder vote to appoint CFO Rahul Bothra and co-founder Phani Kishan Addepalli to the company’s board.

The appointments will not take place now.

The proposal was part of changes to the Articles of Association (AoA) that would have given Swiggy founder and CEO Sriharsha Majety the right to appoint a board member from the management team - in this case, Bothra.

Phani Kishan was expected to take the board seat previously held by co-founder Nandan Reddy, who left the company earlier this year.

Swiggy’s management needed 75% shareholder approval for the resolution to pass, but received only 72.36% votes in favour.

Swiggy’s remaining major pre-IPO investors (like Prosus, SoftBank, and Accel) voted in favour of the management.

However, it appears mutual funds tipped the balance against the proposal. MFs collectively own over 20% in Swiggy.

items.title: RT Deedy: The vibes in SF feel pretty frenetic right now. The divide in outcomes is the worst I've ever seen. Over the last 5yrs, a group of ~10k peop...
items.description_html:
RT Deedy
The vibes in SF feel pretty frenetic right now. The divide in outcomes is the worst I've ever seen.

Over the last 5yrs, a group of ~10k people - employees at Anthropic, OpenAI, xAI, Nvidia, Meta TBD, founders - have hit retirement wealth of well above $20M (back of the envelope AI estimation).

Everyone outside that group feels like they can work their well-paying (but <$500k) job for their whole life and never get there.

Worse yet, layoffs are in full swing. Many software engineers feel like their life's skill is no longer useful. The day to day role of most jobs has changed overnight with AI.

As a result,
1. The corporate ladder looks like the wrong building to climb.
Everyone's trying to align with a new set of career "paths": should I be a founder? Is it too late to join Anthropic / OpenAI? should I get into AI? what company stock will 10x next? People are demanding higher salaries and switching jobs more and more.

2. There’s a deep malaise about work (and its future).
Why even work at all for “peanuts”? Will my job even exist in a few years? Many feel helpless. You hear the “permanent underclass” conversation a lot, esp from young people. It's hard to focus on doing good work when you think "man, if I joined Anthropic 2yrs ago, I could retire"

3. The mid to late middle managers feel paralyzed.
Many have families and don't feel like they have the energy or network to just "start a company". They don't particularly have any AI skills. They see the writing on the wall: middle management is being hollowed out in many companies.

4. The rich aren’t particularly happy either.
No one is shedding tears for them (and rightfully so). But those who have "made it" experience a profound lack of purpose too. Some have gone from <$150k to >$50M in a few years with no ramp. It flips your life plans upside down. For some, comparison is the thief of joy. For some, they escape to NYC to "live life". For others still, they start companies "just cuz", often to win status points. They never imagined that by age 30, they'd be set. I once asked a post-economic founder friend why they didn't just sell the co and they said "and do what? right now, everyone wants to talk to me. if i sell, I will only have money."

I understand that many reading this scoff at the champagne problems of the valley. Society is warped in this tech bubble. What is often well-off anywhere else in the world is bang average here.

Unlike many other places, tenure, intelligence and hard work can be loosely correlated with outcomes in the Bay. Living through a societally transformative gold rush in that environment can be paralyzing. "Am I in the right place? Should I move? Is there time still left? Am I gonna make it?" It psychologically torments many who have moved here in search of "success".

Ironically, a frequent side effect of this torment is to spin up the very products making everyone rich in hopes that you too can vibecode your path to economic enlightenment.

items.title: RT New York Magazine: Joe Lim estimates that 90 percent of what you see on the internet is advertising in disguise, and he should know. For three year...
items.description_html:
RT New York Magazine
Joe Lim estimates that 90 percent of what you see on the internet is advertising in disguise, and he should know. For three years, Lim ran a company called Floodify, which at its peak operated 65,000 dummy social-media accounts used to drum up attention on behalf of paying clients.

The point of this kind of marketing is that nobody is supposed to notice it. But lately, the machinery has started to show.

In April, Justin Bieber headlined two consecutive weekends at Coachella. Coachella is the biggest stage in pop music save only for the Super Bowl, the kind of event that in theory generates its own attention. And yet on both weekends, a Discord server writer Lane Brown had been monitoring hosted paid campaigns for Bieber’s Coachella performances, offering clippers — people who are hired to turn a song, trailer, interview, stump speech, or whatever into short, social-media-friendly fragments — as much as a dollar per thousand views.

“On social media, popular opinion is being formed, measured, and manipulated all at once, and every signal the platforms produce — a trending song, a backlash, a talking point, the feeling that ‘everybody’ is suddenly talking about the same thing — can now be fabricated by unseen actors with hidden agendas,” writes Brown.

“Everybody is doing this now,” Lim says. “And if you’re not, you’re behind.”

Brown reports on how the same techniques are now being used to fool people on every app they go to in order to find out what other people think, not just in music but across entertainment, politics, consumer products, and celebrity gossip: https://nymag.visitlink.me/w6Bu9N

items.title: Rapido’s overall round is $730 million, of which $240 million is primary capital. This implies a massive secondary sale of nearly $500 million by exi...
items.description_html:
Rapido’s overall round is $730 million, of which $240 million is primary capital.

This implies a massive secondary sale of nearly $500 million by existing investors - one of the largest private market exits over the last 18 months.

Swiggy sold 12% stake in Rapido for over $250 million.

It is likely that Rapido's early investors are also exiting, fully or partially, given that Rapido is now an 11-year-old company.


The Arc: 🚨 Rapido has raised $240 mn in funding at $3 bn valuation - a significant jump from $1.1 bn valuation in Sep 2024.

Venture firm Accel has joined as the new investor. The round was led by existing backer Prosus.

Rapido is taking on Uber in India's ride-hailing market.

items.title: Project Vault—a hybrid futures market & strategic reserve for critical minerals—should require mandatory participation with fees scaled to firm size...
items.description_html: Project Vault—a hybrid futures market & strategic reserve for critical minerals—should require mandatory participation with fees scaled to firm size, fund against worst-case collective supply shocks, & prioritize processed materials over raw ore. https://www.piie.com/publications/policy-briefs/2026/strengthening-project-vault-us-plan-stockpile-critical-minerals

items.description_html:
New @Trade__Talks episode: @arvindsubraman explains to @ChadBown how poorer countries are being squeezed by China’s failure to grow out of many industries important to their economic development.
Listen here or wherever you get podcasts: https://tradetalkspodcast.com/podcast/215-how-chinas-trade-is-holding-back-developing-countries/
